theme: The Sculptor in the Remote District
text:
In a remote district on a windswept peninsular, there lived an obscure sculptor whose true identity remained as elusive as an obscure rumour. His apartment, adjacent to a damp cavern near the coast, was a strange institution unto itself. Behind a waterproof curtain he kept the basis of his craft: a spectrum of stones, a pitcher of water, a rack of tools, and a wide pink membrane of wax he would melt and sprinkle over each frame.

He did not belong to any collective, nor did he beg the mayor or the senate for favours. Customary opinion held that he would degrade his gift if he let a financier appraise it, peg a price to it, or strip it of meaning by selling it alongside cheap goods. Yet his sculptures could exemplify an entire sequence of human feeling, from reverence to bitterness, with perceptible clarity. People said a single piece could obliterate doubt and link the viewer to something eternal.

The story goes that the ruling pope once contemplated a vote in the local senate to glorify the man, and a Russian collector offered to pay a surcharge so steep it could remedy the debt of a small town. But the sculptor would not be a pawn. He refused obedience to fashion. 'Will you eject your own conscience for gold?' he asked, and the financier could only gape, his thigh trembling, before he beg pardon and left.

Frequently, as the hours would elapse, the artist sat behind his bench and let his hand sew the final seam of a clay cloak, or sift fine dust through a sieve. A small burrow of mice lived in the wall, and a holocaust of moths once swarmed his lamp. None of this would obscure his focus. For the sake of the work, he would emit no complaint, blot out no error, and overlook no defect.

One customary morning a great airliner roared overhead, its multiplication of engines loud enough to pounce on the silence. The vibration shook a thing from the shelf. The handling of grief, he believed, was the true construction of art. He had no horsepower, no leadership title, no schooling beyond what the woods taught him; even so, an applicant for apprenticeship arrived weekly. Whichever pupil proved competent, he would teach the alphabet of stone. Interference from the outside world, the customary holocaust of noise and rumour, could not strip away the quiet reverence in which he worked, even as the years melted by. He once gave me a pill for my cough and spoke in a single grave utterance about the macroeconomics of art, how a liberal patron might export beauty abroad. I postulate, he said, that fame is insubstantial; therewith he would countermand any order for the delivery of his work to a gallery of mere conquest. The mucous damp of the cave clung to my tibia as I left, while the ejection of hot wax along a circuit of moulds was his only farewell.
translation:
在一个饱受风吹的半岛上的偏远地区，住着一位默默无闻的雕塑家，他真实的身份像一则晦涩的传闻一样难以捉摸。他的公寓紧邻海边一处潮湿的洞穴，本身就是一所奇异的机构。在一道防水的帘子后面，他保存着手艺的根基：各色石料、一只水罐、一架工具，以及一大片宽阔的粉色蜡膜——他会把蜡熔化，喷洒在每一具骨架上。

他不属于任何集体，也不向市长或参议院乞求恩惠。按照惯常的看法，倘若他让金融家来评估作品、给它标个价，或把它与廉价货摆在一起出售而剥夺其意义，他便糟蹋了自己的天赋。然而他的雕塑能以可感的清晰，举例呈现人类情感的一整个系列，从崇敬到辛酸。人们说，单单一件作品就能抹去疑虑，把观者与某种永恒之物连接起来。

据说当政的教皇曾考虑在地方参议院投票来颂扬此人，一位俄罗斯收藏家也愿意支付一笔高昂的额外费用，足以弥补一座小镇的债务。但这位雕塑家不愿做棋子。他拒绝向时尚屈服。「你愿为黄金喷出自己的良心吗？」他问道，金融家只能张口结舌，大腿发颤，随后求得原谅便离开了。

常常，随着时间流逝，这位艺术家坐在工作台后，让手缝合一件黏土斗篷的最后一道接缝，或用筛子筛过细尘。墙里住着一窝老鼠的地洞，曾有大群飞蛾扑向他的灯。这些都无法使他分心。为了作品，他不发一句怨言，不抹去一处败笔，也不忽略任何瑕疵。

一个寻常的清晨，一架巨大的客机从头顶轰鸣而过，多重引擎的声音大得足以扑灭寂静。震动把一样东西从架上摇落。他相信，对悲伤的处理才是艺术真正的构造。他没有马力，没有领导头衔，除了树林教给他的之外没有受过学校教育；即便如此，每周都有人前来申请当学徒。无论哪个学生证明自己能胜任，他都会教他石头的字母表。来自外界的干扰，那惯常的噪音与传闻的大肆侵扰，都无法剥夺他工作时那份静默的崇敬，哪怕岁月正在融化般流逝。 他曾给我一粒药丸治咳嗽，并以一句庄重的话谈起艺术的宏观经济学，谈起一位慷慨的赞助人如何可能把美输出到国外。他说，我假定名声是虚无缥缈的；据此他会撤回任何把他作品送往一座只为征服而设的画廊的订单。当我离开时，洞穴里黏液般的潮气贴着我的胫骨，而热蜡沿着一圈模具的喷射，便是他唯一的告别。
